steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 1 claims it is obvious that UNESCO’s selection boosted awareness and tourism. Text 2 pushes back with "However": cities must create intentional marketing strategies or visitors won’t even know about the designation. The question asks what Text 2’s author would most likely say in response to that underlined claim.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Many people worked together to help Belo Horizonte be recognized by UNESCO, but chefs deserve the most credit for Belo Horizonte's selection as a City of Gastronomy.: Text 2 never discusses who deserves credit for the selection. ✗
  • Choice B — Belo Horizonte may have benefited from being selected by UNESCO, but tourism can likely be further increased by making more potential visitors aware of the city's status as a City of Gastronomy.: This concedes some benefit while arguing that awareness requires active promotion — exactly Text 2’s argument. ✓
  • Choice C — The naming of Belo Horizonte as a City of Gastronomy was long overdue, given the city's unique and delicious food.: Text 2 says nothing about the timing of the selection. ✗
  • Choice D — The benefits of being named a City of Gastronomy extend well beyond tourism.: Text 2 focuses narrowly on tourism and marketing; it does not claim broader benefits. ✗
That is the correct answer.
